comment_text.sn35.246:1.1: The same five defilements are listed at MN 151:3.3.
comment_text.sn35.246:1.2: “Poor condition” is duhitika, cf. Sanskrit durhita (Rig Veda 8.19.26; Atharva Veda 4.36.9). See too SN 42.9:2.1 and note, and the opposite suhita (“replete”) at MN 3:3.1, etc.
comment_text.sn35.246:3.3: The ghaṭā (“pot”) is the prominent hump of a zebu bull, which is grabbed to this day by bull wranglers.
comment_text.sn35.246:3.14: For “straightened up”, accept MS reading udujita, resolved as ud + √uj + ita.
comment_text.sn35.246:4.1: The “arched harp” (vīṇa) was a multi-stringed instrument held in the lap, whose form is clear from representations in ancient Indian sculptures in Bharhut, Amarāvatī, and Pawāyā, as well as the coins of Samudragupta. The strings, typically seven in number, were attached to the base of the soundbox and passed through holes in the parchment resonator to be tied with cords to the curved neck. It accompanied song or dance, and was played equally by men and women. It is not the modern Indian instrument called veena, which is a lute or stick zither, but rather is similar to the Burmese saung.
comment_text.sn35.246:4.17: Most of these were established by Ananda K. Coomaraswamy, “The Parts of a Vīnā”, Journal of the American Oriental Society, Vol. 50 (1930), pp. 244–253. “Soundbox” is doṇi (“tub”); “leather resonator” is camma, a piece of leather stretched over the soundbox to enhance the resonance; “neck” is daṇḍa, the curved arm or frame to which the strings were attached; “strings” is tantiyo; and “plectrum” is koṇa. The remaining part is variously spelled as upaviṇe, upadhiraṇe, upadhāraṇe, or upaveṇaṁ, suggesting an early corruption. The preponderance of plural forms nonetheless supports Coomaraswamy’s revised understanding (“The Parts of a Vīṇā”, JAOS, Vol. 57, No. 1 (March 1937), pp. 101–103) that this is Sanskrit upavīṇa, upavāṇa, or upaveṇa having the sense “bindings”. This would be the “tuning cords” by which the strings are tied to the neck and which also serve for tuning.
comment_text.sn35.246:4.18: Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.4.9 says you can only catch the sound of a harp by getting hold of the lute or player, using this as a metaphor for the search for the Self. DN 23:19.3 tells a similar parable, only with a horn. The Upaniṣad has both instruments.
comment_text.sn35.246:4.24: The rare verb samanvesati is used in SN 4.23:10.4 and SN 22.87:11.5 of Māra “searching” for the consciousness of a deceased arahant.

root_text.sn35.246:1.1: “Yassa kassaci, bhikkhave, bhikkhussa vā bhikkhuniyā vā cakkhuviññeyyesu rūpesu uppajjeyya chando vā rāgo vā doso vā moho vā paṭighaṁ vāpi cetaso, tato cittaṁ nivāreyya.
root_text.sn35.246:1.2: Sabhayo ceso maggo sappaṭibhayo ca sakaṇṭako ca sagahano ca ummaggo ca kummaggo ca duhitiko ca.
root_text.sn35.246:1.3: Asappurisasevito ceso maggo, na ceso maggo sappurisehi sevito.
root_text.sn35.246:1.6: yassa kassaci, bhikkhave, bhikkhussa vā bhikkhuniyā vā jivhāviññeyyesu rasesu …pe…
root_text.sn35.246:1.7: manoviññeyyesu dhammesu uppajjeyya chando vā rāgo vā doso vā moho vā paṭighaṁ vāpi cetaso tato cittaṁ nivāreyya.
root_text.sn35.246:1.8: Sabhayo ceso maggo sappaṭibhayo ca sakaṇṭako ca sagahano ca ummaggo ca kummaggo ca duhitiko ca.
root_text.sn35.246:1.9: Asappurisasevito ceso maggo, na ceso maggo sappurisehi sevito.
root_text.sn35.246:2.2: Kiṭṭhārakkho ca pamatto, goṇo ca kiṭṭhādo aduṁ kiṭṭhaṁ otaritvā yāvadatthaṁ madaṁ āpajjeyya pamādaṁ āpajjeyya;
root_text.sn35.246:2.3: evameva kho, bhikkhave, assutavā puthujjano chasu phassāyatanesu asaṁvutakārī pañcasu kāmaguṇesu yāvadatthaṁ madaṁ āpajjati pamādaṁ āpajjati.
root_text.sn35.246:3.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, kiṭṭhaṁ sampannaṁ kiṭṭhārakkho ca appamatto goṇo ca kiṭṭhādo aduṁ kiṭṭhaṁ otareyya.
root_text.sn35.246:3.3: Nāsāyaṁ suggahitaṁ gahetvā uparighaṭāyaṁ suniggahitaṁ niggaṇheyya.
root_text.sn35.246:3.4: Uparighaṭāyaṁ suniggahitaṁ niggahetvā daṇḍena sutāḷitaṁ tāḷeyya.
root_text.sn35.246:3.7: tatiyampi kho, bhikkhave, goṇo kiṭṭhādo aduṁ kiṭṭhaṁ otareyya.
root_text.sn35.246:3.9: Nāsāyaṁ suggahitaṁ gahetvā uparighaṭāyaṁ suniggahitaṁ niggaṇheyya.
root_text.sn35.246:3.10: Uparighaṭāyaṁ suniggahitaṁ niggahetvā daṇḍena sutāḷitaṁ tāḷeyya.
root_text.sn35.246:3.12: Evañhi so, bhikkhave, goṇo kiṭṭhādo gāmagato vā araññagato vā, ṭhānabahulo vā assa nisajjabahulo vā na taṁ kiṭṭhaṁ puna otareyya—
root_text.sn35.246:3.14: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, yato kho bhikkhuno chasu phassāyatanesu cittaṁ udujitaṁ hoti sudujitaṁ, ajjhattameva santiṭṭhati, sannisīdati, ekodi hoti, samādhiyati.
root_text.sn35.246:4.1: Seyyathāpi, bhikkhave, rañño vā rājamahāmattassa vā vīṇāya saddo assutapubbo assa.
root_text.sn35.246:4.4: ‘ambho, kassa nu kho eso saddo evaṁrajanīyo evaṅkamanīyo evaṁmadanīyo evaṁmucchanīyo evambandhanīyo’ti?
root_text.sn35.246:4.6: ‘esā, kho, bhante, vīṇā nāma, yassā eso saddo evaṁrajanīyo evaṅkamanīyo evaṁmadanīyo evaṁmucchanīyo evambandhanīyo’ti.
root_text.sn35.246:4.11: ‘ayaṁ kho sā, bhante, vīṇā yassā eso saddo evaṁrajanīyo evaṅkamanīyo evaṁmadanīyo evaṁmucchanīyo evambandhanīyo’ti.
root_text.sn35.246:4.13: ‘alaṁ me, bho, tāya vīṇāya, tameva me saddaṁ āharathā’ti.
root_text.sn35.246:4.15: ‘ayaṁ kho, bhante, vīṇā nāma anekasambhārā mahāsambhārā.
root_text.sn35.246:4.17: doṇiñca paṭicca cammañca paṭicca daṇḍañca paṭicca upadhāraṇe ca paṭicca tantiyo ca paṭicca koṇañca paṭicca purisassa ca tajjaṁ vāyāmaṁ paṭicca evāyaṁ, bhante, vīṇā nāma anekasambhārā mahāsambhārā.
root_text.sn35.246:4.19: So taṁ vīṇaṁ dasadhā vā satadhā vā phāleyya, dasadhā vā satadhā vā taṁ phāletvā sakalikaṁ sakalikaṁ kareyya.
root_text.sn35.246:4.20: Sakalikaṁ sakalikaṁ karitvā agginā ḍaheyya, agginā ḍahitvā masiṁ kareyya.
root_text.sn35.246:4.21: Masiṁ karitvā mahāvāte vā ophuneyya, nadiyā vā sīghasotāya pavāheyya.
root_text.sn35.246:4.23: ‘asatī kirāyaṁ, bho, vīṇā nāma, yathevaṁ yaṁ kiñci vīṇā nāma ettha ca panāyaṁ jano ativelaṁ pamatto palaḷito’ti.
root_text.sn35.246:4.24: Evameva kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu rūpaṁ samanvesati yāvatā rūpassa gati, vedanaṁ samanvesati yāvatā vedanāya gati, saññaṁ samanvesati yāvatā saññāya gati, saṅkhāre samanvesati yāvatā saṅkhārānaṁ gati, viññāṇaṁ samanvesati yāvatā viññāṇassa gati.
root_text.sn35.246:4.25: Tassa rūpaṁ samanvesato yāvatā rūpassa gati, vedanaṁ samanvesato …pe…
root_text.sn35.246:4.29: Yampissa taṁ hoti ahanti vā mamanti vā asmīti vā tampi tassa na hotī”ti.

translation_text.sn35.246:1.1: “Mendicants, any monk or nun who has desire or greed or hate or delusion or repulsion come up for sights known by the eye should shield their mind from them:
translation_text.sn35.246:1.2: ‘This path is dangerous and perilous, thorny and tangled; it’s a wrong turn, a bad path, in poor condition.
translation_text.sn35.246:1.3: This path is frequented by untrue persons, not by true persons.
translation_text.sn35.246:1.5: The mind should be shielded from this when it comes to sights known by the eye.
translation_text.sn35.246:1.6: Any monk or nun who has desire or greed or hate or delusion or repulsion come up for sounds … smells … tastes … touches …
translation_text.sn35.246:1.7: ideas known by the mind should shield their mind against them:
translation_text.sn35.246:1.8: ‘This path is dangerous and perilous, thorny and tangled; it’s a wrong turn, a bad path, a sorry state.
translation_text.sn35.246:1.9: This path is frequented by untrue persons, not by true persons.
translation_text.sn35.246:1.11: The mind should be shielded from this when it comes to ideas known by the mind.
translation_text.sn35.246:2.2: but the caretaker is negligent. If an ox fond of crops invades the crops they’d indulge themselves as much as they like.
translation_text.sn35.246:2.3: In the same way, when an unlearned ordinary person doesn’t exercise restraint when it comes to the six fields of contact, they indulge themselves in the five kinds of sensual stimulation as much as they like.
translation_text.sn35.246:3.1: Suppose the crops have ripened, and the caretaker is diligent. If an ox fond of crops invades the crops
translation_text.sn35.246:3.2: the caretaker would grab them firmly by the muzzle.
translation_text.sn35.246:3.3: Then they’d grab them above the hump and hold them fast there.
translation_text.sn35.246:3.4: Then they’d give them a good thrashing
translation_text.sn35.246:3.7: and even a third time, the same thing might happen.
translation_text.sn35.246:3.12: As a result, no matter how often they might stand or sit in a village or wilderness, that ox fond of crops would never invade that crop again,
translation_text.sn35.246:3.13: remembering the beating they got earlier.
translation_text.sn35.246:3.14: In the same way, when a mendicant’s mind is straightened up, well straightened up when it comes to the six fields of contact, becomes stilled internally; it settles, unifies, and becomes immersed in samādhi.
translation_text.sn35.246:4.1: Suppose a king or their chief minister had never heard the sound of an arched harp.
translation_text.sn35.246:4.2: When he first hears the sound,
translation_text.sn35.246:4.4: ‘Oh my, what is making this sound, so arousing, sensuous, intoxicating, infatuating, and captivating?’
translation_text.sn35.246:4.8: ‘Go, mister, fetch me that arched harp.’
translation_text.sn35.246:4.13: ‘I’ve had enough of that arched harp! Just fetch me the sound.’
translation_text.sn35.246:4.15: ‘Sir, this arched harp is made of many components assembled together,
translation_text.sn35.246:4.16: which make a sound when they’re played. That is,
translation_text.sn35.246:4.17: it depends on the soundbox, the leather resonator, the neck, the tuning cords, the strings, the plectrum, and a person to play it properly. That’s how an arched harp is made of many components assembled together,
translation_text.sn35.246:4.18: which make a sound when they’re played.’
translation_text.sn35.246:4.19: But he’d split that harp into ten pieces or a hundred pieces, then splinter it up.
translation_text.sn35.246:4.20: He’d burn the splinters with fire, and reduce them to ashes.
translation_text.sn35.246:4.21: Then he’d whisk away the ashes in a strong wind, or glide them down a swift stream.
translation_text.sn35.246:4.23: ‘It seems that there’s nothing to this thing called an arched harp or whatever’s called an arched harp! But people waste their time with it, negligent and heedless!’
translation_text.sn35.246:4.24: In the same way, a mendicant searches for form, feeling, perception, choices, and consciousness anywhere they might be reborn.
translation_text.sn35.246:4.25: As they search in this way,
translation_text.sn35.246:4.29: their ideas of ‘I’ or ‘mine’ or ‘I am’ are no more.”

variant_text.sn35.246:4.23: yathevaṁ yaṁ → yathevayaṁ (pts1ed) | ettha ca panāyaṁ jano → ettha panāyaṁ jano (sya-all, km); ettha ca mahājano (pts1ed, mr)
